rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF MAURICIO ANTONIO VARELA CARIAS
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Authorizing And Directing Clerk Of The Court To File Portions Of First And Final Report And Account Of Former Successor Conservator Of The Estate And Petition For Its Allowance And Approval; For Confirmation Of All Acts Of Former Successor Conservator; For An Award Of Conservator'S Guardian Ad Litem'S And Attorneys' Fees And Reimbursement Of Costs Advanced; For Exoneration Of Former Successor Conservator'S Bond; And For Final Discharge Of Former Successor Co - (Added To Calendar For The Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only) -
rulings.Rulings: Part 2 of 2 Unnamed Physician v. Bd. of Trustees of Saint Agnes Med. Ctr. (2001) 93 Cal. App. 4th 607 is inapposite. There, a physician claimed a reputational right to mask their name in a pre-adjudication challenge to a hospital disciplinary proceeding. The court found a cognizable interest at the pre-adjudication stage. The court did not address a request to seal any pleadings, nor did it apply the standard articulated in Rule 2.550. Moving party is directed to file a complete unredacted version of all papers related to the account and lodge courtesy copies of same, if he has not already done so, no later than noon on October 6, 2020. Thomas is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on October 1, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. Part 2 of 2

rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF MAURICIO ANTONIO VARELA CARIAS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Authorizing And Directing Clerk Of The Court To File Portions Of First And Final Report And Account Of Former Successor Conservator Of The Estate And Petition For Its Allowance And Approval; For Confirmation Of All Acts Of Former Successor Conservator; For An Award Of Conservator'S Guardian Ad Litem'S And Attorneys' Fees And Reimbursement Of Costs Advanced; For Exoneration Of Former Successor Conservator'S Bond; And For Final Discharge Of Former Successor Conservator/Guardian Ad Litem Under Seal
rulings.Rulings: Part 1 of 2 This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Before the court is Former Conservator of the Estate Herb Thomas' Motion to Seal Portions of First and Final Account of Former Successor Conservator of the Estate and Petition for Its Allowance and Approval; for Confirmation of All Acts of Former Successor Conservator; for an Award of Conservator's, Guardian ad Litem's and Attorney's Fees and Reimbursement of Costs Advanced; for Exoneration of Former Successor Conservator's Bond; and for Final Discharge of Former Successor Conservator/Guardian ad Litem. The motion is DENIED. The motion is denied as procedurally defective. Thomas failed to file a proof of service. Further, Thomas failed to submit a declaration in support of the motion. (See Cal. Rules of Court, rule 2.551 ["The motion or application [to seal] must be accompanied by a memorandum and a declaration containing facts sufficient to justify the sealing."].) Independently, the motion is denied on the merits. Unless confidentiality is required by law, court records are presumed to be open. The court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish: (1) there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Court, Rule CRC 2.550(d) ); see NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218.) Here, the court cannot expressly find facts that establish each of the required elements. Thomas has not established a substantial probability that the conservatee will be prejudiced any more than any other conservatee by being subject to a conservatorship accounting based on a record open to public scrutiny. Risk of financial fraud or abuse is no greater in this case than other conservatorships; in any event, the court has put in place a conservatorship of the estate for the very purpose of protecting the conservatee from fraud or abuse and the court would not have appointed the conservator in this case if it doubted their ability to execute on this mission. Additionally, the public accounting process-the very process that would be stymied by the requested sealing order-is a key tool for guarding against financial fraud and abuse. Nor has Thomas established that it is substantially probable that the conservatee's interest in the civil settlement will be materially prejudiced if the records are not sealed. The court finds that the settling defendants' interest in sealing does not override the public interest in open records in conservatorship proceedings and, in particular, this conservatorship proceeding. The parties' agreement that the record should be sealed does not control. (See Cal. Rules of Court, Rule 2.551(a) ["The court must not permit a record to be filed under seal based solely on the agreement or stipulation of the parties."].) Nor is the requested sealing order narrowly tailored. It would result in concealing from public view critical information presented in the accounting without any showing of a cognizable confidentiality interest, let alone a confidentiality interest that overrides the public interest in open accountings and open court records. Also, it would result in concealing such things as service fees that are, at best, tangentially linked to the articulated confidentiality interests. Part 1 of 2
